purpose: Claim ledger for the Fable 5 pipeline mission (docs/_handoff/2026-06-11-fable5-mission-prompt.md). Doctrine: optimistic intake (ASSUMED) -> verification (VERIFIED / PARTIAL / REFUTED) -> deepen (gsr_application). Nothing ASSUMED or REFUTED may enter the build plan. This file is the research loop's heartbeat; commit it every cycle.

status_legend.ASSUMED: heard from a source, not yet checked
status_legend.VERIFIED: confirmed against official docs/changelog/live test (evidence cited)
status_legend.PARTIAL: works with caveats (caveat stated in verifier_evidence)
status_legend.REFUTED: checked and false/stale; never build on it

claims.claim: Supabase background job queue pattern: create a table (e.g. background_jobs) with status (pending/processing/completed/failed) and payload columns. API route inserts a row and returns immediately. A Supabase Edge Function fetches one pending row, sets it to processing, runs the heavy logic (AI calls, PDF generation, etc.), then updates to completed/failed. pg_cron triggers the edge function every minute to poll for pending jobs.

claims.gsr_application: GSR's RC import, lower-thirds generation, and AI script extraction are all slow operations that would benefit from this queue pattern to avoid Vercel timeout limits and keep the dashboard UI responsive.

claims.claim: Custom sub-agents can be stored in three scopes: (1) ./.claude/agents/ for project-level shared with the team, (2) ~/.claude/agents/ for personal use across all projects, (3) via plugin system for distribution. claims.claim: The Claude Code task system exposes four tools to agents: TaskCreate, TaskGet, TaskList, TaskUpdate. Both primary agents and sub-agents can use these tools to coordinate work. The task system activates by default when a prompt is large enough and the model is Opus.

claims.claim: Hook trigger matching in settings.json can only match on tool type (Write, Edit, Bash), not on specific commands or file patterns. To filter further (e.g., only fire on mkdir, only on .md files), the hook script itself must inspect the incoming command/path and exit early if not relevant.

claims.claim: MCP servers load all tool descriptions into the context window upfront, making them context-heavy. claims.claim: Claude Agent SDK can be invoked from a FastAPI web server with skills loaded from a local .claude/skills/ directory. claims.claim: Claude Agent SDK overhead is approximately 28,000 input tokens per call regardless of prompt size. Each agent run costs roughly 30-60 cents at API rates. Three env vars redirect the SDK to any OpenAI-compatible provider: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL (no /v1 suffix for Anthropic-compatible endpoint), ANTHROPIC_API_KEY='' (blank to override stored login), ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_MODEL (model name). The SDK auto-appends a '1m' context-size suffix to model names.

claims.claim: Skill YAML frontmatter supports a disable_model_invocation field (default false). claims.claim: Remote Claude Code routines clone the linked GitHub repository into a cloud environment at run time, execute the prompt, then destroy that environment. Any file changes the routine writes (memory files, logs, trade journals) must be committed and pushed back to the repo during the run, or the next routine invocation will not see them. The routine settings include a permission toggle called 'allow unrestricted branch pushes' that must be enabled for Claude to push to any branch.

claims.gsr_application: GSR routines that update episode state, lower-thirds drafts, or research notes must include an explicit push-back step in the routine prompt, and the 'allow unrestricted branch pushes' permission must be on, or state will be lost between runs.

claims.claim: API keys for remote Claude Code routines must be stored as named environment variables in the cloud environment configuration (set in the Claude desktop app under the environment's settings), not in a .env file inside the repo. The environment variable names in the routine prompt must match exactly letter-for-letter the names configured in the environment, or the routine cannot find them.
claims.source: video 6MC1XqZSltw I Turned Claude Into a 24/7 Trader
claims.gsr_application: GSR's Supabase, Rundown Creator, and Anthropic API keys for remote routines must be entered in the cloud environment settings panel, not in any .env committed to the GitHub repo, and the routine prompt must reference them by exact variable name.

claims.claim: Claude Design is a new Anthropic Labs product (research preview as of June 2026) for building prototypes, slide decks, and one-pagers via natural language. It is powered by Opus 4.7. It supports a design system feature where brand assets and guidelines are ingested once and applied to all subsequent projects. Finished designs can be handed off to Claude Code via a generated copy command that fetches the design file as a zip and implements it in a local project. Exports include Canva, PDF, PowerPoint, and HTML.
claims.source: video gAoZ95kqG7w Claude Design Just Became Unstoppable
claims.gsr_application: GSR could use Claude Design to prototype lower-thirds layouts, slide templates for internal presentations, or show graphics concepts before handing them off to Claude Code for implementation, keeping everything in the Anthropic ecosystem.

claims.claim: The author's practical heuristic for choosing between n8n and Claude Code: if a use case can be built in n8n in 10 minutes or less, build it there because publishing, error workflows, execution logs, and triggers are already configured. Use Claude Code for complex iterative builds or tasks that need deep reasoning. The two tools are complementary, not substitutional, and existing n8n automations do not need to be migrated.
claims.source: video wb4uDdsFh6o Is n8n Dead?
claims.gsr_application: Reinforces CL-024 with an actionable decision rule: GSR's simple webhook-triggered notifications or data-sync automations belong in n8n; the pipeline stages requiring multi-step reasoning or file manipulation belong in Claude Code.

claims.claim: claude remote-control (or /remote-control in existing session) generates a QR code and URL to control a local Claude Code session from any browser or phone. Session stays entirely local -- phone is just a window. Requires Pro or Max plan (not team/enterprise yet). Connection drops after ~10 minutes without internet; laptop must stay on and terminal must stay open.
claims.source: video EqhKw0Oro_k Claude Code Just Added What Everyone Wanted (Remote Control)
claims.verifier_evidence: Documented at https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/remote-control
claims.gsr_application: Daniel could start a GSR pipeline task at his desk and monitor or steer it from his phone while away, without needing a separate cloud agent setup.

claims.claim: Skills live at .claude/skills/<skillname>/skill.md with YAML front matter (name, description fields) followed by step-by-step instructions. Supporting scripts and reference files can be nested directly under the skill folder or stored elsewhere in the project -- as long as the skill.md points to their path. Skills can call sub-agents and external scripts.
claims.source: video zKBPwDpBfhs Master 95% of Claude Code Skills in 28 Minutes
claims.verifier_evidence: Consistent with Anthropic Claude Code skills documentation structure
claims.gsr_application: Defines the canonical file structure for GSR production skills (lower-thirds extraction, RC import, script generation) so Daniel can build them without guessing layout.

claims.claim: Scheduled tasks (desktop app only, not terminal or VS Code extension) are stateless -- each run is a fresh session with no context from prior runs. The cron metadata lives in the desktop app's global folder. Desktop app must stay open; if missed, the app looks back 7 days and catches up. Use /schedule in an existing session or the Schedule tab in the desktop app to create.
claims.source: video BlNJFa3Btm8 Claude Code 2.0 Is Finally Here
claims.gsr_application: GSR Studio v4 five-hour scheduled Claude resume task must use the desktop app and account for statelessness -- each task should read a status file from the prior run to maintain continuity.

claims.claim: /loop uses three internal tools: cron_create (schedule), cron_list (list active), and cron_delete (cancel). Loop jobs are per-session -- a cron_list in session A cannot see loops created in session B. Closing the terminal or re-opening a conversation tab kills all loops for that session. Default interval is 10 minutes if none specified.
claims.source: video OUyfxhFtGCo This New Claude Code Feature is a Game Changer
claims.gsr_application: For short-duration GSR monitoring tasks (e.g., check RC for new rows every 10 min during a shoot day), /loop is the right tool; for persistent daily jobs use scheduled tasks.

claims.claim: Agent teams are enabled by adding CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS to .claude/settings.local.json (not settings.json). Teammates inherit all permissions from the main session. Teammates can access all project files, MCP servers, and skills. Running in tmux allows direct messaging to individual teammate terminals. Recommended team size is 3-5 agents; larger swarms multiply cost proportionally.
claims.source: video vDVSGVpB2vc How to Build Claude Agent Teams Better Than 99% of People
claims.gsr_application: If Daniel ever runs a multi-stage GSR pipeline (research + script + RC import simultaneously), agent teams with file ownership per agent prevents overwrite collisions.

claims.claim: Auto Dream is a background sub-agent that consolidates and prunes Claude memory .md files. Access via /memory, toggle 'auto dream on', manually invoke with /dream. Only touches memory files (never code/scripts). Triggers based on elapsed hours or session count (exact thresholds unconfirmed). Reviewing what it changed: it reports which files were updated, entries added, and sections removed.
claims.source: video LrgfmZkl3nc Claude Code Just Dropped Memory 2.0
claims.gsr_application: Auto Dream can keep GSR project memory files compact and accurate across Daniel's 5-10 parallel sessions, reducing context bloat in the MEMORY.md-heavy workflow.

claims.claim: Auto mode (claude --enable-auto-mode) runs a classifier before every tool call to detect risky actions (destructive commands, sensitive data). Safe actions execute automatically; risky ones are blocked and Claude tries an alternative approach. It is more expensive than normal mode because of the per-call classifier. Currently research preview, available to Claude team plan users only; org admin must enable it under organization settings > Claude Code > auto permissions mode.
claims.source: video pkSxISewcw8 STOP Using Bypass Permissions, Use This New Feature Instead
claims.gsr_application: Auto mode is the recommended middle path for unattended GSR pipeline runs -- safer than dangerously skip permissions without the constant approval interruptions of ask-before-edits.

claims.claim: Claude Code channels are distinct from remote control and dispatch: Dispatch = message-based task delegation (send task, Claude runs locally); Channels = event-based push from Telegram/Discord/iMessage into a running session (best for reacting to external events); Remote Control = direct steering of an active session from another device. iMessage channel requires bun, Mac full disk access granted to the terminal/VS Code, and launching Claude with the channel plugin active. Enterprise/team users need admin to enable channels.
claims.source: video hHlpVeooPrI Claude Code + iMessage is Finally Here.
claims.gsr_application: The Dispatch mechanism (not channels) is the right choice for GSR -- Daniel can send a task from his phone to a running local session without setting up a full iMessage bridge.

claims.claim: Each connected MCP server loads all tool definitions into context on every message -- one MCP server can add approximately 18,000 tokens per message. The prompt cache has a 5-minute timeout: stepping away longer than 5 minutes causes the next message to reprocess the entire context from scratch at full cost. /compact should be run manually at ~60% context capacity rather than waiting for auto-compact at 95%. Peak usage hours (8am-2pm ET weekdays) drain the 5-hour session allocation faster.
claims.source: video 49V-5Ock8LU 18 Claude Code Token Hacks in 18 Minutes
claims.gsr_application: Daniel runs 5-10 parallel sessions with multiple MCPs (Supabase, RC, Gmail, Vercel) -- disconnecting unused MCPs between tasks could recover tens of thousands of tokens per session.

claims.claim: /ultra plan (keyword or /ultra plan <prompt> in CLI only -- not desktop app or VS Code extension) sends the planning session to Anthropic's cloud where it runs on Opus 4.6 with 3 parallel exploration agents plus 1 critique agent. Requires a git repo synced to the project. 30-minute cloud cap. The plan can be reviewed and commented on in the claude.ai web UI before being teleported back to the local terminal. Ultra plan completed a dashboard build in ~10-15 min vs ~45 min for local plan mode in a direct comparison.
claims.source: video T4fXb3sbJIo Planning In Claude Code Just Got a Huge Upgrade
claims.gsr_application: For complex GSR architecture decisions (e.g., designing the RC-to-Supabase import pipeline), /ultra plan could produce a higher-quality plan faster than local plan mode, at the cost of requiring a git remote.

claims.claim: Anthropic Managed Agents (in Claude Console) charge $0.08/hour per active session plus token costs; idle environments do not accrue charges. Managed agents have no native cron scheduling -- they must be triggered by an external API call. Credentials are stored in shareable vaults (OAuth-based). Upcoming features (not yet available): outcomes (self-evaluation loop), multi-agent orchestration via callable agents tool, and persistent memory across sessions.
claims.source: video 27Y44JYXZJ8 I Tested Claude's New Managed Agents... What You Need To Know
claims.gsr_application: Managed Agents are not the right fit for GSR automation (no native cron, needs external trigger glue) -- reinforces continuing with Claude Code desktop scheduled tasks or trigger.dev patterns instead.

claims.claim: Routines cloud environment resource limits: 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, 30 GB disk per run. Network access levels are: trusted (vetted Anthropic-approved domains only), full (any outbound), none, or custom (specific domains). A setup script can run before Claude Code launches in a routine. Per-day limits: Pro=5, Max=15, Team/Enterprise=25; orgs with extra usage enabled can exceed the cap on metered overage.
claims.source: video ehg4fhydTgs Claude Code Finally Gave Us Scheduled Automations
claims.gsr_application: GSR routines for guest research, lower thirds generation, and RC sync now have a concrete resource envelope to design against; trusted mode blocks non-Anthropic domains so API calls to RC or Supabase may require full or custom mode.

claims.claim: Routine branches pushed to GitHub are preserved after the run; the cloned environment is destroyed but the session history (run logs) also persists. Routines can be triggered three ways: schedule, API call (webhook), or GitHub event (PR, push, issue, release). Each routine uses its own prompt injected into a fresh Claude Code session reading the connected GitHub repo.
claims.source: video ehg4fhydTgs Claude Code Finally Gave Us Scheduled Automations
claims.gsr_application: GSR could trigger RC-sync or episode-processing routines via webhook from Basecamp or a Vercel deploy hook rather than only on a fixed schedule.

claims.claim: /btw (also /by the way) opens a quick overlay for side questions that do NOT enter the conversation history, keeping context clean while still getting an answer.
claims.source: video _qZvORxGqI0 How to Manage Your Claude Limits Better Than 99% of People
claims.gsr_application: Daniel can ask quick mid-session questions about RC column IDs or episode numbers without polluting his main context window.

claims.claim: Prompt caching TTL: on a Claude subscription = 1 hour. On API or metered extra-usage overage = 5 minutes by default (can be bumped to 1 hour at extra cost). Sub-agents on any plan = 5 minutes TTL. Switching the model mid-session (including the 'model Opus plan' flag which toggles Opus in plan mode and Sonnet in execution) breaks the cache because each model has its own cache. Editing CLAUDE.md mid-session does NOT break cache -- the edit applies only on next session restart.
claims.source: video 6cEQEba0i2A The One Habit That Doubles Your Claude Code Session Limit
claims.gsr_application: GSR multi-session pipeline work: staying on one model throughout a session preserves cache; subagents used for RC or Supabase calls reset their own 5-min TTL, so frequent subagent invocations are more expensive than expected.

claims.claim: /rewind has a 'summarize from here' option that generates a handoff message summarizing what was decided, for use as context in a fresh session after /clear.
claims.source: video _qZvORxGqI0 How to Manage Your Claude Limits Better Than 99% of People
claims.gsr_application: Replaces manual summary step when chaining GSR pipeline sessions (research -> scoring -> script prep).

claims.claim: In the terminal (not desktop app), /clear preserves active cron jobs (loops); the desktop app kills crons on /clear. Crons are implemented via three internal tools: cron_create, cron_list, cron_delete. Desktop app shows loops expiring in 3 days; terminal documentation says 7 days.
claims.source: video xJ5oz63mIec I Tested 3 Ways to Deploy Claude Agents
claims.gsr_application: GSR loops monitoring RC for new rundown rows or checking Supabase for pending extractions should be run from the terminal to survive /clear context resets during long sessions.

claims.claim: Desktop app local scheduled tasks catch up after the machine was off -- on relaunch, all tasks that missed their scheduled fire times execute in sequence. To prevent catch-up, pause tasks before turning off the machine.
claims.source: video xJ5oz63mIec I Tested 3 Ways to Deploy Claude Agents
claims.gsr_application: GSR local scheduled tasks (e.g. overnight episode prep) will replay if Daniel's Mac was off; tasks that should NOT replay (one-shots) must be paused before shutdown.

claims.claim: Claude Agent SDK is stateless by default (each call = fresh session with no memory). Passing the session_id in the request makes it stateful -- the SDK resumes that session. The SDK auto-compacts but does not issue /clear; to start fresh, create a new session. Monthly subscription credit can go toward Agent SDK usage but as a separate dedicated budget, not the same weekly rolling window (announced May 13 2026).
claims.source: video xJ5oz63mIec I Tested 3 Ways to Deploy Claude Agents
claims.gsr_application: GSR automation scripts calling the Agent SDK for RC sync or lower-thirds generation must pass session_id to maintain context across pipeline stages; otherwise each call is blind.

claims.claim: /ultra review requires Claude Code >= v2.1.86 and a logged-in Claude account (API key alone is insufficient). It uploads the branch to a cloud sandbox, spins up parallel reviewer agents that each attack from a different angle (logic, security, performance, edge cases), and only reports bugs that are independently reproduced and verified. Pro and Max plans get 3 free runs; subsequent runs cost approximately $5-20 per run depending on codebase size. Runs take 10-20 minutes but execute in the background.
claims.source: video eRS3CmvrOvA I Tried 100+ Claude Code Skills. These 6 Are The Best
claims.gsr_application: Before merging GSR dashboard changes touching Supabase RLS policies, lower-thirds import logic, or RC API integration, /ultra review catches verified bugs without false positives.

claims.claim: Context Mode plugin routes tool call output (bash, URL fetch, Playwright snapshots) through a sandbox subprocess; only a compressed summary enters the context window. Published benchmarks: 56KB Playwright snapshot -> 299 bytes in context; 46KB access log -> 155 bytes; 315KB raw output over a full session -> 5KB. Tracks all file edits, tasks, decisions, and errors in a local SQLite database and rebuilds a session snapshot on compaction. Install: two commands to add plugin + MCP server, then restart Claude Code. Check stats with /contextmode:ctx-stats.
claims.source: video eRS3CmvrOvA I Tried 100+ Claude Code Skills. These 6 Are The Best
claims.gsr_application: GSR sessions reading large Supabase query results or RC row dumps would have their context blown out by raw JSON; Context Mode would compress these to summaries before they pollute the window.

claims.claim: Dynamic workflows (released with Opus 4.8) have Claude generate a JavaScript file that orchestrates parallel subagents. The file is saved to .claude/workflows/ and can be re-run. /workflows command browses history and monitors running workflows. ultra code effort level = xhigh + workflows (every prompt defaults to a workflow). /deep research automatically invokes a dynamic workflow. The trigger keyword was changed from workflow to ultra code shortly after release to prevent accidental workflow invocations. Workflows confirm before running; the user must explicitly approve.
claims.source: video jZgcWCzxh1I Claude Code Dynamic Workflows Clearly Explained
claims.gsr_application: GSR could use /deep research to do multi-source adversarial research on guest credentials or episode topics, spinning up parallel agents and voting on each claim.

claims.claim: Opus 4.8 effort levels in Claude Code (terminal): low, medium, high (default), xhigh, max, ultra code (xhigh+workflows). ultra code is the highest tier -- every prompt is routed through a dynamic workflow. Rate limits for API usage of Claude Code were increased with Opus 4.8 to accommodate higher token usage at elevated effort levels (does not affect the weekly session limit, only API rate limits).
claims.source: video q5lg3npxjAc Opus 4.8 Just Dropped. Heres How To Actually Use It.
claims.gsr_application: Confirms the /effort slider in CL-032; ultra code is a new sixth tier above max that auto-invokes workflows. Daniel's existing GSR sessions at default high effort can be tuned down for simple lower-thirds runs and up for research-heavy sessions.

claims.claim: Custom subagent files live in .claude/agents/ as markdown files with YAML front matter containing: name, description (the trigger/progressive-disclosure selector), model, color (visual indicator in terminal), memory (project/user/local/none -- recommended: project), tools (allowlist), disallowed_tools (denylist), and MCP server restrictions. max_turns parameter limits loop depth. Claude reads only the front matter to decide whether to invoke; full body only loads on invocation (progressive disclosure). claude --agent-name launches a session directly as that subagent. Project-level agents (.claude/agents/) are repo-scoped; global-level agents (~/.claude/agents/) are available across all projects.
claims.source: video e18sdZLwP7o How to Build Claude Subagents Better Than 99% of People
claims.gsr_application: GSR could build a dedicated rc-importer agent (read-only, Haiku model, max_turns=5) and a supabase-query agent -- each invoked automatically by the main session without re-explaining context, with explicit tool denylist preventing destructive operations.

claims.claim: Agent View navigation: left arrow in any session opens agent view; right arrow enters a session; /bg pushes the current session to agent view; claude --bg task-description launches a new session directly into agent view. Ctrl+X twice kills a selected session in agent view. Replies can be sent to waiting sessions directly from agent view without entering them.
claims.source: video ZAaxx3qyT8g Multi-Agent Building In Claude Code Somehow Got Easier
claims.gsr_application: Daniel can manage 3-5 parallel GSR pipeline sessions (research, scoring, RC editing, lower-thirds generation) from one agent view pane, replying to sessions needing approval without switching terminal tabs.

claims.claim: Claude Fable = Claude Mythos 5 with additional cyber guardrails, available on subscription June 9-22 2026, then switching to usage credits. Priced at $10/million input tokens and $50/million output tokens -- 2x more expensive than Opus on input, more on output. Safeguards configured aggressively at launch and will be tuned based on community feedback.
claims.source: video 8QQ_INxAhRs I Turned Claude Fable Into The Ultimate Second Brain
claims.gsr_application: Fable would consume Daniel's $200/month Max plan session limit significantly faster than Opus; not recommended for routine GSR pipeline work (lower thirds, RC sync) but potentially valuable for deep guest research requiring multi-step reasoning.

claims.claim: /insights command generates an HTML report on the past 30 days of Claude Code usage: session counts, turn counts, token spend by model and project, patterns, quick wins, and ambitious workflows it recommends based on observed behavior. Auto memory (formerly auto dream) automatically updates CLAUDE.md memory files on an interval without user prompting; enable via the memory settings in Claude Code.
claims.source: video vfWTyEreOEc I Tested Every Claude Code Feature, These 12 Are the Best
claims.gsr_application: Daniel can run /insights monthly to spot which GSR pipeline phases consume the most tokens and identify automation opportunities; auto memory reduces the manual CLAUDE.md upkeep burden.

claims.claim: Printing Press (printingpress.dev) is a CLI factory and library: ~50 pre-built Go-based CLIs (ESPN, YouTube, Tally, Shopify, Craigslist, etc.) plus a factory skill to generate custom CLIs from any API or website. Requires Go installed. Benchmark claim from video: MCP used 35x more tokens than equivalent CLI on same task; MCP reliability dropped from 100% to 72% as tasks got harder vs CLI staying at 100%. CLI output is compressed -- 132K tokens of raw data returns only ~2K token summary to context.
claims.source: video YHk45NEpspE Printing Press Just 10xd Everyones Claude Code
claims.gsr_application: A custom Rundown Creator CLI via Printing Press could replace the RC MCP server, reducing per-call token overhead significantly and eliminating MCP tool-definition context bloat on every GSR session.
